name: llm_eval_v1
description: MVP benchmark for LLM-refiner evaluation. Single coherent scenario: fictional Loomflux engineering project tracked across 6 sessions over 36 weeks. 24 memories; 16 hand-curated queries across four classes (code_recall, multi_hop, cross_session, decayed_recall).
scenario: A small engineering team builds an audit-log pipeline. Roles change (Diana departs at week 18), tools evolve (PostgreSQL -> ClickHouse), and code decisions accumulate. Queries test single-fact recall, chained reasoning, state-evolution awareness, and pre-supersession recall.

memories.content: Loomflux kickoff at week 0. Anika Rao proposed using PostgreSQL with logical replication as the storage backend for the audit log; the team approved this at the kickoff meeting. The decision was logged in ADR-001.
At kickoff Diana Park was assigned as data infrastructure lead. She owned the audit log pipeline, including schema design, replication topology, and the ingest workers in src/ingest/pg_writer.rs.
Sven Halligan came on as ops lead at kickoff. He set up the initial monitoring stack (Prometheus + Grafana) and wrote the deploy scripts under deploy/k8s/.

At week 4 the team shipped the MVP audit-log pipeline. PostgreSQL ingest rate measured at 8,000 events/second sustained. Latency p95 under 80ms. Diana wrote up the postmortem on the early-week schema corruption incident.

Week 4: the score_pairs function in src/retrieval/scorer.py was originally a naive cosine bi-encoder over MiniLM embeddings. It was correctly returning numeric scores but the ranking quality was middling.
Anika decided at week 4 that the retrieval layer would use bcrypt for any stored credential hashes. She updated src/auth/hasher.rs to use bcrypt with cost factor 12.
Mateo Cruz joined the team at week 10 as the ML/retrieval engineer. His first task was overhauling the retrieval scoring path that Diana had inherited from the kickoff phase.

Week 10: Mateo replaced the naive bi-encoder in score_pairs with the ms-marco-MiniLM-L-6-v2 cross-encoder. Recall@10 on the internal benchmark improved from 0.61 to 0.78. The change shipped in PR #142.

At week 10 Anika introduced a feature flag system (src/flags/mod.rs) gated by environment variable LOOMFLUX_FLAGS. The retrieval rewrite shipped behind the rerank_v2 flag, defaulting off in production until the canary completed.

Sven set up a weekly cadence at week 10 for ops review: every Thursday 10am the team reviews paging events and uptime. The first review surfaced that the audit-log replica lag was creeping up under peak load.

Week 18: Diana Park left Loomflux to take a role at a different company. Her ownership of the audit-log pipeline transferred to Mateo Cruz. Mateo inherited the schema, the replication topology, and the ingest workers.

After taking over the audit-log pipeline at week 18, Mateo proposed migrating from PostgreSQL to ClickHouse because the write throughput on PostgreSQL had hit a hard ceiling at 14,000 events/second. The team approved the migration in ADR-012.

Week 18: Anika rewrote the auth-hasher module to use Argon2id instead of bcrypt, citing the OWASP 2025 recommendation. The change in src/auth/hasher.rs preserves legacy bcrypt verification during a 6-month migration window.

Week 18 retrospective: Sven noted that paging volume had dropped 40% since the rerank_v2 flag was fully enabled at week 14. The retrieval-quality improvement reduced downstream noisy alerts on bad recommendations.

Week 25: the ClickHouse migration completed. Audit-log ingest now sustained at 42,000 events/second with latency p95 under 30ms. Mateo wrote up the migration retrospective and archived ADR-001 (the original PostgreSQL decision) as superseded by ADR-012.

At week 25 Anika added structured logging via tracing-subscriber across all services. Log format standardized to JSON. The integration was straightforward except for src/auth/hasher.rs where the spans needed to be careful not to log password material.

Sven proposed at week 25 that the team adopt error budgets formally. SLO set at 99.9% for the audit-log pipeline and 99.5% for the retrieval API. The first month of operation showed both well above target.

Week 25 code change: score_pairs in src/retrieval/scorer.py was extended with a tags_match boost factor (weighted at 0.18) drawn from a tag-weighted Jaccard between query and document tags. Mateo's PR #287.

Week 36 current state: Mateo is the lead engineer for both the audit-log pipeline (ClickHouse) and the retrieval API. The retrieval API serves p95 latency under 45ms and Recall@10 holds at 0.83 on the production-traffic benchmark.

Week 36: Anika is rolling out a new feature flag, retrieval_v3, that introduces a graph-traversal stage before scoring. Early canary numbers show a 6% Recall@10 lift but higher tail latency. Decision on full rollout deferred to week 40.

Week 36 ops: Sven reports that the audit-log pipeline has had zero pages in the last six weeks. The error budget remains untouched. He's redirecting effort to capacity planning for the projected Q4 traffic doubling.

Week 36 documentation: the public-facing engineering wiki now lists the authoritative database choice for the audit log as ClickHouse, with a footnote crediting Diana for the original PostgreSQL design and Mateo for the migration. The wiki page link is internal: wiki/audit-log/architecture.

Week 36: Mateo merged PR #401 which changed score_pairs to delegate cross-encoder scoring to a separate worker pool via tokio::spawn_blocking, reducing main-thread starvation. Mean retrieval latency dropped 12% in canary.

At week 36 Anika finalized the security review for the Argon2id migration. The bcrypt verification path was removed; src/auth/hasher.rs now only accepts Argon2id-hashed credentials. Stragglers on bcrypt were force-rotated.

queries.query: Which cross-encoder model replaced the naive bi-encoder in score_pairs?
What file holds the credential hashing implementation, and what algorithm does it use now?
Which PR moved cross-encoder scoring to a separate worker pool?
Where is the feature flag system defined?
Who took over the audit-log pipeline after Diana left, and what database does it use now?
Why did we migrate the audit log to ClickHouse, and what was the throughput before the migration?
Who introduced the rerank_v2 flag and which engineer's improvement was shipped behind it?
What was the dropped paging volume attributed to, and which engineer was the source of that improvement?
What is the current state of our audit-log infrastructure?
What's the latest with the retrieval API?
How has Anika's work on credential hashing evolved across the project?
Summarize the current ownership of major components at Loomflux.
What database did Loomflux originally pick for the audit log at the week-0 kickoff?
Which hashing algorithm did the auth module use before the Argon2id migration?
What was the implementation of score_pairs before Mateo joined?
Who originally owned the audit-log pipeline before the week-18 transition?
